description: Automotive ECU relevance mappings for MITRE ATT&CK ICS techniques

T0800.automotive_context: CAN bus messages can be replayed to repeat actuator commands (e.g. torque, braking) without driver input.
examples: Replay captured brake torque release command
Re-inject accepted OTA update packages

T0801.automotive_context: Attacker modifies calibration or sensor data in ECU memory — e.g. altering torque limits or safety thresholds.
examples: Overwrite EEPROM calibration via UDS WriteDataByIdentifier
Modify torque limit parameters

T0802.automotive_context: Attacker activates automated routines (e.g. calibration, diagnostic modes) outside their intended context.
examples: Trigger calibration routine via UDS RoutineControl while vehicle is moving

T0803.automotive_context: Attacker blocks or corrupts diagnostic communication to prevent fault detection or OTA updates.
examples: Flood OBD-II port with ISO-TP frames to deny UDS sessions
DoS DoIP gateway during OTA

T0806.automotive_context: Attacker repeatedly sends I/O commands via CAN to force an actuator beyond its safety limits.
examples: Brute force PWM duty cycle commands on inverter output stage

T0807.automotive_context: Attacker injects a malicious command message impersonating a trusted ECU on the CAN bus.
examples: Inject forged CAN frame with VCU arbitration ID to send torque command
Spoof UDS tester present message

T0809.automotive_context: Attacker tampers with data stored in non-volatile memory (EEPROM, Flash) of an ECU.
examples: Corrupt NVM calibration data
Flip security seed in EEPROM to bypass authentication

T0811.automotive_context: Attacker reads diagnostic or sensor data from the ECU to map system behaviour before an attack.
examples: Read ECU VIN, SW version and calibration IDs via UDS ReadDataByIdentifier
Enumerate CAN bus message IDs by passive listening

T0820.automotive_context: Attacker exploits ECU firmware vulnerability to bypass security checks or disable monitoring.
examples: Buffer overflow in CAN message parser to disable intrusion detection

T0821.automotive_context: Attacker abuses a trusted network path (e.g. OBD tester, telematics, V2X) to reach internal ECUs.
examples: Pivot from OBD dongle via gateway to powertrain ECU
Exploit telematics unit to reach CAN bus

T0826.automotive_context: Attacker floods the CAN bus or automotive ethernet to prevent legitimate ECU messages from being processed, causing loss of control.
examples: CAN bus flood to deny safety-critical torque commands
Ethernet storm on SOME/IP network

T0828.automotive_context: Attacker interferes with sensor data (e.g. position sensor, temperature) causing the ECU to make incorrect control decisions.
examples: Inject false rotor position values to cause inverter phase fault
Spoof temperature sensor reading to bypass thermal protection

T0829.automotive_context: Attacker uses a connected device or legitimate service channel to target a deeper ECU via the network.
examples: Use compromised infotainment unit to send CAN frames to powertrain network

T0830.automotive_context: Attacker impersonates a trusted node on the CAN bus or automotive ethernet to inject commands or drain data.
examples: Spoof VCU node ID to send forged torque commands to inverter
Man-in-the-middle on SOME/IP service discovery

T0831.automotive_context: Attacker manipulates the firmware update process (OTA or via UDS) to flash malicious code.
examples: Inject unsigned firmware via UDS RequestDownload / TransferData
MITM OTA session to substitute firmware package

T0836.automotive_context: Attacker modifies actuator commands in transit on the CAN bus — e.g. changing torque or braking commands.
examples: Intercept and modify torque request frames on CAN-FD
Alter ABS command signals in transit

T0837.automotive_context: Attacker exploits weak or missing security seed/key exchange in UDS security access to unlock privileged services.
examples: Brute force UDS SecurityAccess seed-key exchange
Exploit weak XOR-based seed algorithm to unlock ECU

T0838.automotive_context: Attacker restores an ECU to a previous (potentially vulnerable) firmware version via downgrade attack.
examples: Flash older firmware version that lacks a security patch
Replay an old OTA package after disabling anti-rollback counter

T0839.automotive_context: Attacker targets supply chain components (libraries, tools, hardware) to embed malicious code before the ECU reaches the vehicle.
examples: Compromised open-source AUTOSAR stack dependency
Malicious bootloader from third-party Tier-2 supplier

T0840.automotive_context: Attacker uses reverse engineering or network capture to extract proprietary calibration data or cryptographic keys from an ECU.
examples: Dump ECU flash via JTAG to extract calibration tables
Power analysis attack to extract session keys

T0845.automotive_context: Attacker modifies the program logic of the ECU microcontroller (e.g. altering interrupt handlers or safety monitors).
examples: Overwrite safety monitor interrupt handler via UDS memory write
Replace AUTOSAR OS task with malicious logic via OTA

T0846.automotive_context: Attacker queries or injects messages on the remote I/O interface (e.g. LIN, K-Line) of a sub-system ECU.
examples: Spoof LIN slave response to inject false sensor data
Inject rogue LIN master command to actuator

T0848.automotive_context: Attacker exploits a rogue device connected to the OBD-II port or USB port to access vehicle networks.
examples: Plug rogue OBD dongle with CAN injection capability
Connect USB device exploiting automotive USB stack

T0849.automotive_context: Attacker manipulates real-time clock or network time signals to invalidate certificate validity or timing-sensitive safety functions.
examples: Spoof GPS time to invalidate TLS certificates in telematics
Corrupt CAN time sync (AUTOSAR TimeSync) to break message scheduling

T0851.automotive_context: Attacker abuses legitimate remote access or diagnostics to send commands from outside the vehicle.
examples: Abuse telematics backdoor to send remote unlock or remote start commands

T0856.automotive_context: Attacker spoofs or replays command messages to trigger unintended actuator behaviour (throttle, brakes, steering).
examples: Spoof torque command on CAN-FD impersonating VCU
Replay valid braking command at wrong time

T0859.automotive_context: Attacker exploits default or stolen credentials to access diagnostic services or telematics backend.
examples: Default UDS seed/key known from public documentation
Hardcoded OTA backend API key extracted from firmware

T0860.automotive_context: Attacker reads or eavesdrops on network traffic to extract sensitive data or learn message formats.
examples: Passive CAN bus sniffing to reverse-engineer message formats
Capture plaintext OTA traffic to extract calibration data

T0861.automotive_context: Attacker modifies point values or sensor signals to cause an ECU to operate outside safe parameters.
examples: Inject false wheel speed signals to disable ABS
Override current sensor reading to bypass overcurrent protection

T0862.automotive_context: Attacker targets automotive supply chain software or hardware components to introduce vulnerabilities before deployment.
examples: Compromised third-party MCAL driver introduced via supplier

T1694.automotive_context: Attacker uses hard-coded or default credentials in diagnostic tools or telematics to gain unauthorized access.
examples: Default seed-key algorithm found in OEM workshop manuals
Hardcoded API key in mobile companion app
